1: Date: twelfth year, twelfth month, first day — March 585 BCE, eight months after Jerusalem's fall. The second lamentation over Pharaoh comes after the catastrophe Ezekiel had prophesied — now the fall of Egypt can be seen in light of the confirmed fall of Jerusalem.
2: sa qinah al-paro ('raise a lamentation over Pharaoh'): the second qinah (H7015, dirge/elegy) for Pharaoh — ch 19's lion/vine dirge for Israel's kings is now applied to Egypt's king. kelefir goyim domita veatta katannim bayammim ('you were like a young lion among the nations, and like a monster in the seas'): tannin (H8577) again — the sea-dragon of ch 29. But the comparison is complex: Pharaoh thought he was a young lion (kephir, H3715, the prime young lion) but was actually the sea-monster that roils the waters — a chaos creature rather than a royal beast.
3: ufarsetti alecha et-reshet biqhal ammim rabbim ('I will throw my net over you with a host of many peoples'): YHWH as fisherman casting a net — the same fishing/netting imagery as 29:4, but now applied to the sea-monster context. The chaos creature caught in the divine net. veheeluka becharemi ('they will haul you up in my net'): the nations as YHWH's fishing tools, hauling up the tannin.
4: venitashticha al-haaretz ('I will throw you on dry land'): the sea-monster removed from its element — the fish out of water. The birds and beasts consuming Pharaoh (v.4) reverse the images of Egyptian religious power: the sacred ibis, vulture, and crocodile, all representations of Egyptian deities, will feed on the corpse of the divine king.
5: venatatti et-besarecha al-heharim ('I will lay your flesh on the mountains'): the empire's vast body (basar, H1320, flesh/body) scattered over mountains and valleys — the geographic distribution of the Egyptian corpse across the landscape. The image of an empire's remains spread across a broad terrain captures both the scale of the defeat and its permanence.
6: vehishqeiti eretz tzafecha midamecha ('I will drench the land with your flowing blood'): the blood that flows from the corpse as a flood — the blood-drenching reverses the Nile flood that made Egypt's agriculture possible. The Nile that gave life through flooding becomes the blood-flood of death. el-heharim umilleu hayaqot ('even to the mountains, the ravines will be filled'): the blood reaches everywhere, the inverse of the Nile's life-giving overflow.
7: uvchasoti bekhavotecha et-shamayim ('when I blot you out I will cover the heavens'): cosmic darkness accompanying Egypt's fall — the darkening of celestial bodies. vehishchakti et-shimsha beanav uyare'ach lo-ya'ir oro ('I will cover the sun with a cloud and the moon will not give its light'): solar and lunar eclipse as divine signs of judgment. This imagery appears in the Day of the LORD texts (Joel 2:10,31; 3:15; Amos 8:9) and in NT eschatological contexts (Matt 24:29; Rev 6:12-13). The darkening of Egypt's lights is both literal (smoke and darkness of conquest) and metaphorical (the end of Egyptian civilization's 'light' to the world).
8: kol-meoroth hameeor bashamayim achasheet alecha ('all the luminous lights of the sky I will darken over you'): the comprehensive darkening — sun, moon, stars, all lights extinguished. Egypt was the civilization of Ra/sun worship; to darken the sun over Egypt is to defeat the deity Egypt trusted. The theological polemic is precise: the god Egypt worshiped cannot protect it; the God Egypt ignored is the one who controls all lights.
9: vekaastu lev ammim rabbim ('I will trouble the hearts of many peoples'): kaas (H3707, provoke/trouble/grieve) — the psychological ripple effect of Egypt's fall. Nations that organized their world around Egyptian power as the regional anchor will be destabilized. el-aratzot asher lo yedatum ('to countries you have not known'): the scope of the trouble extends beyond Egypt's immediate neighbors to distant lands — a universal shock.
10: vehashimoti alecha ammim rabbim ('I will appall many peoples over you'): shamem (H8074, be desolate/appalled) — the same word for the land's desolation applied here to the nations' psychological response. vemalkeihem yisaaru alecha sareer ('and their kings will shudder with horror when I brandish my sword before them'): saar (H8175, shudder/shiver/tremble) — visceral fear in the rulers when they see the sword of judgment fall on the greatest empire of the age.
11: cherev melek bavel tevoaka ('the sword of the king of Babylon will come against you'): the explicit naming of the human instrument after the extended cosmic imagery (vv.7-10). The celestial phenomena of darkness and the political reality of the Babylonian military are two descriptions of the same event: YHWH's judgment executed through history.
12: vehippilti hamonech bechorvot gibborim araritzei goyim culam ('I will cause your multitude to fall by the swords of the mighty — the most ruthless of nations, all of them'): gibborim (H1368, mighty warriors/heroes) — the elite warriors; araritzei goyim again. vegadevu et-gon mitzraim ('they will destroy the pride of Egypt'): gaon mitzraim — Egypt's pride, the word used in 30:6. The pride that characterized Egypt from v.3 ('my Nile — I made it') is the specific target of the judgment's destruction.
13: vehibadeti et-kol-behemtah mealy mayim rabbim ('I will destroy all its livestock from beside the great waters'): the pastureland fed by the Nile — Egypt's agricultural wealth dependent on the river's overflow. velo tedolel maregel adam od ('no human foot will trouble them anymore'): the silence after destruction — no more footsteps in the devastated land, no more cattle-hooves disturbing the river. The peace of desolation.
14: az ashiq meimehem veneharehem kashesmen azilem ('then I will let their waters settle and cause their rivers to flow like oil'): the paradoxical peace of the post-judgment Nile — waters no longer muddied by Pharaoh's thrashing (cf. 29:3), running smooth as oil. The oracle that began with Pharaoh muddying the waters (32:2) ends with the waters cleared of his disturbance.
15: beten et-eretz mitzraim shemamah ('when I make the land of Egypt desolate'): the recognition formula anchors the chapter's conclusion — the cosmic darkness, the military defeat, the silenced waters: all serve the epistemological purpose of YHWH's self-disclosure. The end of Egypt as a great power is the means by which the nations will know the LORD.
16: qinah hi veqonenuha ('this is the lamentation they will sing over her'): the meta-statement about the oracle's genre, identical in structure to 19:14 ('this is a lamentation and has been used as a lamentation'). The daughters of the nations (benot goyim) sing the lamentation — the mourning women of antiquity who formalized communal grief. Egypt receives the same funeral rite that a great city deserves, pronounced by the nations over whom it once exercised power.
17: A third date within the Egypt section: twelfth year, fifteenth of the month (month unspecified — likely the same twelfth month, two weeks after v.1). The descent-to-Sheol oracle (vv.17-32) is the most extended underworld passage in the OT outside of Isaiah 14.
18: ben-adam naleh el-hamon mitzraim ('son of man, wail for the multitude of Egypt'): naleh (H5098, wail/growl/roar) — a different and stronger word than standard mourning verbs; the prophet's wailing drags Egypt down. vehored otah ve-et-benot goyim adamot ('bring them down — both Egypt and the daughters of the majestic nations'): the command to bring nations down to Sheol is prophetic speech-act — the oracle performs what it describes. The mourning song of the prophet is simultaneously the vehicle of descent.
19: mimi naamt redi vehashshkab et-arelim ('whom do you surpass in beauty? Go down and lie with the uncircumcised'): the contempt of the invitation — Egypt's beauty (naom, H5278, pleasantness/beauty) is dismissed; the invitation to lie with the uncircumcised is ironic hospitality. arel (H6189, uncircumcised) — in the Sheol roll-call that follows, 'uncircumcised' is the mark of those who died in disgrace outside the covenant community. For Egypt to be uncircumcised is also ironic: Egyptian males were circumcised (Herodotus 2.36-37; Gen 34:9-10), yet the theological uncircumcision of their hearts (Jer 9:25-26; Ezek 44:7-9) places them with the covenant-excluded.
20: betoch chalalei-cherev yipolu ('they will fall among those slain by the sword'): the military death as the entry point to Sheol. cherev nitanah meshochahu vekol-hamoneiha ('Egypt is delivered to the sword — draw her down with all her multitude'): the sword as the agent of the descent, delivering Egypt to Sheol along with its entire hamon (multitude/wealth).
21: yedaberu-lo elei gibborim mitoch sheol ('the mightiest warriors will speak to him from the midst of Sheol'): the underworld as a place of ongoing speech — the shades of mighty warriors addressing the newly-arrived Pharaoh. This is the most developed OT picture of Sheol as a realm with inhabitants (cf. Isa 14:9-11 where Sheol's rulers rise to greet the king of Babylon). mehelai-cherev yeredu shachvu arelim ('slain by the sword, they have gone down, lying uncircumcised'): the entrance formula for Sheol in this passage.
22: sham ashur vechal-qahalah ('Assyria is there with all its company'): the great empire catalog begins — Assyria, which fell to Babylon (612 BCE), is already in Sheol. The Assyrian army's graves surround it — the dead empire retains its military formation in death, but now it is a formation of graves, not fighting units. The parade of fallen empires makes the theological point: no nation that has lorded its power over others escapes the common fate.
23: qivroteiha beyrketei-bor ('its graves are in the depths of the Pit'): bor (H953, pit/cistern/dungeon) — the underworld pit. The Assyrian army's graves in the depths, 'gathered around its tomb' — even in death, the military structure is maintained as a grotesque formation of corpses. asher-nateenu chittit bearetz chayyim ('who had spread terror in the land of the living'): the refrain that appears for each nation — their power was measured by the terror they induced; in Sheol, that terror is silenced.
24: sham elam vechal-hamonah ('Elam is there with all its multitude'): Elam, the ancient Mesopotamian kingdom east of Babylon — its bowmen were famous in antiquity (Isa 22:6; Jer 49:34-39). arelim yoredei eretz tachetit ('uncircumcised, gone down to the depths of the earth'): the shame-formula — lying in Sheol among the uncircumcised, despite having spread terror. The equation of military might with shame in death is the chapter's central theological move.
25: The description of Elam's grave-bed betoch chalalim ('among the slain') with graves surrounding it. veyutenat mishkavah vetoch chalalim — the ignominious grave 'among the slain' is the opposite of honorable burial (kingly tombs, family burial sites). Despite the terror spread among the living, the powerful end up in a common pit with the violently slain.
26: sham meshech tuval vechal-hamonah ('Meshech and Tubal are there with all their multitude'): the northern peoples of Gen 10:2 (sons of Japheth) — often identified with areas in Asia Minor (Phrygia/Cappadocia). Their terror too is silenced in Sheol. These are the same peoples of Gog (Ezek 38-39), completing the catalog of northern, eastern, and western powers.
27: velo yishkevu et-gibborim nophlim mearelim ('do they not lie with the fallen warriors of old who went down to Sheol with their weapons?'): the 'warriors of old' (nophlim me-arelim or gibborim meolam) — possibly a reference to the ancient heroes/Nephilim of Gen 6:4. The ancient warriors went to Sheol with their weapons, weapons placed under their heads and shields covering their bones — honorable warrior burial. But Meshech and Tubal lie differently: not as honored warriors but as those who spread terror without receiving the honor of proper warrior burial.
28: veattah betoch arelim tishaver ('you too will be shattered among the uncircumcised'): Pharaoh, addressed directly, joins the roll-call. The catalog moves from third-person descriptions (Assyria, Elam, Meshech-Tubal, Edom) to second-person address: you will be shattered among them. The roll-call is not a historical survey but an address to the living king — this is what awaits you.
29: sham edom melacheha vechal-nesieiha ('Edom is there — its kings and all its princes'): Edom, despite being south of Israel rather than north, is included. The catalog is arranged by symbolic weight rather than geography. Edom as the perpetual enemy of Jacob/Israel (from Gen 25 onward, through the Esau-Jacob conflict) receives no more special treatment in Sheol than any other nation. bechol-geburtam ('despite their might'): the great leveling — might does not exempt from Sheol's common shame.
30: sham nasichei tzaphon kulam vesidoni ('there are all the princes of the north and all the Sidonians'): the 'princes of the north' = Aramean kingdoms; Sidon = Phoenicia. The fear they inspired (chetit mig'vurtam, 'terror from their might') is gone; they lie in shame with the slain. The geography of the roll-call — Assyria (northeast), Elam (east), Meshech-Tubal (north), Edom (south), northern princes and Sidon (northwest) — encompasses the entire known world around Egypt. Pharaoh will be joined by every regional power.
31: otam yireh paro venecham ('when Pharaoh sees them he will be comforted over all his multitude'): dark comfort — Pharaoh will find consolation in seeing that all the great powers share his fate. The comfort of shared shame is the only comfort Sheol offers. nacham (H5162, comfort/console) — the word used for genuine divine consolation (Isa 40:1; 51:12) here inverted: the comfort of the underworld is merely that misery has company.
32: ki-natati chitti bearetz chayyim ('for I spread his terror in the land of the living'): YHWH acknowledges that Pharaoh's terror (chitti, from chat, H2844, terror) was real — but it was YHWH who gave it to him, and YHWH who now withdraws it. The final clause veyushkav et-paro vekhol-hamono bein arelim ('Pharaoh and all his multitude will be laid among the uncircumcised') closes the entire four-chapter Egypt section with the verdict: despite greatness, power, beauty, and terror, Egypt's end is the common grave of the uncircumcised. The claim 'my Nile — I made it' (29:3) ends in a grave among those outside the covenant.
